title: Artemis Mission – Young Children (Ages 6–8)

hunks.text: The ARTEMIS MISSION is a big plan to send ASTRONAUTS back to the MOON. NASA, which is America's SPACE AGENCY, started this mission to explore the Moon again after many years. Artemis is named after a GODDESS from old stories who was the sister of Apollo. The first Artemis mission will be very exciting for people all around the world.

questions.question: What is the name of America's space agency?
questions.answer: NASA

questions.question: Who is Artemis named after?
questions.answer: A GODDESS FROM OLD STORIES / THE SISTER OF APOLLO

questions.question: Why might NASA want to explore the Moon again?
questions.answer: TO LEARN MORE / TO VISIT A PLACE VISITED BEFORE / TO DISCOVER NEW THINGS

questions.question: The Moon is Earth's natural SATELLITE. Name one thing you know about the Moon.
questions.answer: IT IS ROUND / IT SHINES AT NIGHT / IT HAS CRATERS / IT IS IN SPACE / IT ORBITS EARTH

questions.question: Close your eyes and picture a ROCKET shooting up into the sky. What color is the rocket you see?
questions.answer: WHITE / SILVER / BLUE / ORANGE / YOUR CHOICE

questions.question: If you could travel to the Moon, what would you want to do there?
questions.answer: ACCEPT ANY ANSWER SHOWING IMAGINATION

hunks.text: The ARTEMIS SPACECRAFT is specially built to carry CREW MEMBERS safely through SPACE. The spacecraft has a CAPSULE where astronauts sit, and a SERVICE MODULE that gives the spacecraft power and oxygen. The capsule is much smaller than an airplane but must protect the astronauts from the dangers of space like extreme cold and radiation. Engineers worked for many years to make sure every part of Artemis would work perfectly.

questions.question: What gives the Artemis spacecraft power and oxygen?
questions.answer: THE SERVICE MODULE

questions.question: Name the part of the spacecraft where astronauts sit.
questions.answer: THE CAPSULE

questions.question: What dangers must the spacecraft protect astronauts from?
questions.answer: EXTREME COLD / RADIATION / DANGEROUS CONDITIONS IN SPACE

questions.question: If it took engineers 5 years to design Artemis and 4 years to build it, how many years did it take altogether?
questions.answer: 9 YEARS

questions.question: Oxygen is what we breathe on Earth. Why do you think astronauts need oxygen in space?
questions.answer: BECAUSE THERE IS NO AIR IN SPACE / BECAUSE THEY NEED TO BREATHE / BECAUSE SPACE DOES NOT HAVE OXYGEN

questions.question: Imagine touching the outside of the Artemis spacecraft when it is in cold space. Describe what that would feel like without actually touching anything cold.
questions.answer: VERY COLD / FREEZING / HARD / SMOOTH / YOUR DESCRIPTION

hunks.text: The LAUNCH of Artemis happens at the KENNEDY SPACE CENTER in Florida. This is where the ROCKET stands tall on the LAUNCH PAD before it blasts off. The countdown before launch is very important because every second must be perfect for safety. When the engines ignite, hot FLAMES shoot out and the rocket rises slowly, then faster and faster into the sky. Thousands of people watch the launch, and many more see it on television or the internet.

questions.question: Where is the Kennedy Space Center located?
questions.answer: FLORIDA

questions.question: What is the tall platform called where the rocket stands before launch?
questions.answer: THE LAUNCH PAD

questions.question: Why is the countdown before launch important?
questions.answer: FOR SAFETY / BECAUSE EVERY SECOND MUST BE PERFECT / TO MAKE SURE EVERYTHING IS READY

questions.question: If 100 people watch the launch in person and 1,000 people watch on television, how many people watch the launch altogether?
questions.answer: 1,100 PEOPLE

questions.question: Rockets need fuel to burn and create energy to fly. What do you think happens when the rocket engines ignite?
questions.answer: FUEL BURNS / HEAT IS CREATED / FLAMES COME OUT / ENERGY IS MADE TO PUSH THE ROCKET UP

questions.question: Imagine hearing the sound of a rocket launching. Describe the sound without using the word loud.
questions.answer: RUMBLING / ROARING / BOOMING / THUNDERING / POWERFUL / SHAKING

hunks.text: Once the rocket is in ORBIT around Earth, the spacecraft prepares for the long JOURNEY to the Moon. This trip takes about three DAYS because the Moon is very far away. The ASTRONAUTS sleep, eat special FOOD, and do experiments during the journey. They must wear SPACESUITS if they leave the spacecraft because there is no air to breathe outside. The view of Earth from space is beautiful, and astronauts often talk about how special it feels.

questions.question: How many days does it take to travel from Earth to the Moon?
questions.answer: ABOUT THREE DAYS

questions.question: What must astronauts wear if they leave the spacecraft?
questions.answer: SPACESUITS

questions.question: What do astronauts do during the journey to the Moon?
questions.answer: SLEEP / EAT SPECIAL FOOD / DO EXPERIMENTS / LOOK AT EARTH / PREPARE FOR LANDING

questions.question: If one astronaut sleeps 8 hours each day during the three-day journey, how many hours of sleep is that altogether?
questions.answer: 24 HOURS

questions.question: There is no air in space. What do you think would happen to a person without a spacesuit in space?
questions.answer: THEY WOULD NOT BE ABLE TO BREATHE / THEY WOULD GET VERY COLD / THEY WOULD BE IN DANGER

questions.question: Close your eyes and imagine floating in the spacecraft above Earth. What do you see through the window?
questions.answer: BLUE EARTH / WHITE CLOUDS / STARS / THE MOON / BLACK SPACE

hunks.text: When the spacecraft gets close to the Moon, the LANDER separates from the main capsule. The LANDER is a special vehicle designed to touch down on the lunar SURFACE. The LUNAR MODULE has powerful ENGINES that slow the spacecraft down so it does not crash. Astronauts carefully control the lander while watching instruments and looking out the windows. Landing on the Moon is difficult and needs perfect timing and skill.

questions.question: What is the special vehicle called that lands on the Moon?
questions.answer: THE LANDER / THE LUNAR MODULE

questions.question: What do the lunar module engines do?
questions.answer: SLOW THE SPACECRAFT DOWN

questions.question: Why do you think landing on the Moon is difficult?
questions.answer: BECAUSE IT NEEDS PERFECT TIMING / BECAUSE IT NEEDS SKILL / BECAUSE THE MOON'S SURFACE IS UNEVEN / BECAUSE GRAVITY IS DIFFERENT

questions.question: If the lander is 30 feet tall and the crater below is 50 feet deep, how much higher is the crater than the lander is tall?
questions.answer: 20 FEET

questions.question: Engines create force that pushes things. How do you think slowing down helps the lander land safely?
questions.answer: A SLOW LANDING IS GENTLER / IT PREVENTS CRASHING / IT GIVES PILOTS TIME TO CONTROL IT

questions.question: Imagine you are sitting in the lunar module as it comes down to the Moon's surface. What sensations might you feel?
questions.answer: VIBRATION / SHAKING / PRESSURE / TENSION / MOVEMENT / SLOWNESS

hunks.text: The first person to walk on the Moon during Artemis will be an EXPLORER making history. ASTRONAUTS will collect ROCKS and DUST samples to bring back to Earth for scientists to study. They will take PHOTOGRAPHS of the Moon's surface and set up INSTRUMENTS to measure temperature and radiation. The FOOTPRINTS left by astronauts on the Moon will last for millions of years because there is no wind to blow them away. This mission will teach us amazing things about the Moon.

questions.question: What will astronauts collect to bring back to Earth?
questions.answer: ROCKS AND DUST SAMPLES

questions.question: How long will footprints stay on the Moon?
questions.answer: FOR MILLIONS OF YEARS

questions.question: What will scientists do with the rocks and dust from the Moon?
questions.answer: STUDY THEM / LEARN ABOUT THE MOON / ANALYZE THEM / DISCOVER NEW THINGS

questions.question: If an astronaut collects 10 rocks and 15 samples of dust, how many total samples are collected?
questions.answer: 25 SAMPLES

questions.question: Wind on Earth changes footprints in sand and dirt. Why won't the Moon's wind change the astronauts' footprints?
questions.answer: THERE IS NO WIND ON THE MOON / THE MOON HAS NO ATMOSPHERE

questions.question: If you could ask a Moon explorer what they learned, what would you want to know?
questions.answer: ACCEPT ANY QUESTION SHOWING CURIOSITY

hunks.text: After exploring the Moon, astronauts return to the LAUNCH MODULE and blast off toward Earth. The journey home takes another three DAYS, and the team prepares the spacecraft for RE-ENTRY into Earth's ATMOSPHERE. Re-entry is dangerous because the spacecraft moves very fast and creates extreme HEAT from friction with the air. A special HEAT SHIELD on the outside protects the astronauts from burning up. When the parachutes open, the spacecraft slows down and floats gently back to Earth.

questions.question: What protects the astronauts from the heat of re-entry?
questions.answer: THE HEAT SHIELD

questions.question: How many days does the journey home take?
questions.answer: ABOUT THREE DAYS

questions.question: Why is re-entry into Earth's atmosphere dangerous?
questions.answer: THE SPACECRAFT MOVES VERY FAST / EXTREME HEAT IS CREATED / FRICTION WITH THE AIR IS INTENSE

questions.question: If the trip to the Moon takes 3 days and the trip back takes 3 days, how many days is the total travel time?
questions.answer: 6 DAYS

questions.question: Friction is what happens when two things rub together and create heat. Why does friction happen during re-entry?
questions.answer: BECAUSE THE SPACECRAFT RUBS AGAINST THE AIR / BECAUSE OF THE ATMOSPHERE / BECAUSE OF SPEED

questions.question: Imagine feeling the spacecraft slow down as parachutes open. Describe that feeling without moving.
questions.answer: GENTLE / FLOATING / DRIFTING / PEACEFUL / SLOWING DOWN / PULLING UPWARD

hunks.text: The Artemis mission shows how HUMANS can explore space and learn new things. The astronauts splash down in the OCEAN and are safely recovered by a team waiting in boats. Scientists and engineers celebrate the success of the mission because it took teamwork and years of planning. The data and samples brought back help us understand the Moon, space, and our place in the UNIVERSE. The Artemis mission inspires children and adults to dream about space exploration.

questions.question: Where do the astronauts splash down when returning to Earth?
questions.answer: THE OCEAN

questions.question: What brings back information to help us understand the Moon?
questions.answer: DATA AND SAMPLES

questions.question: What made the Artemis mission successful?
questions.answer: TEAMWORK / PLANNING / HARD WORK / ENGINEERS AND SCIENTISTS / YEARS OF PREPARATION

questions.question: If the Artemis mission involved 500 scientists and 300 engineers, how many total people worked on it?
questions.answer: 800 PEOPLE

questions.question: The universe is all of space and everything in it. Name three things that are part of the universe.
questions.answer: THE MOON / THE SUN / STARS / EARTH / PLANETS / ASTEROIDS / SPACECRAFT

questions.question: How does the Artemis mission inspire you to think about space and exploration?
questions.answer: ACCEPT ANY ANSWER SHOWING PERSONAL CONNECTION AND INSPIRATION

citations: NASA. (2024). Artemis Program Overview. Retrieved from https://www.nasa.gov/artemis/
NASA. (2024). Moon to Mars Exploration Program. Retrieved from https://www.nasa.gov/moon-to-mars/
Kennedy Space Center. (2024). Artemis Mission Information for Educators. Retrieved from https://www.kennedyspacecenter.com/
